text: Melatonin is a hormone that signals the body's sleep-wake cycle; supplements are most effective for circadian-rhythm problems and jet lag.
For sleep onset, doses of 0.5 to 5 mg taken 30 to 60 minutes before bed are typical; lower doses are often as effective as higher ones.
Short-term melatonin use is generally well tolerated; side effects can include daytime drowsiness, headache, and dizziness.
Melatonin may interact with anticoagulants, immunosuppressants, diabetes medications, and blood-pressure drugs.
Magnesium is an essential mineral involved in nerve and muscle function; the glycinate form is well tolerated and gentle on the stomach.
Adequate magnesium status is associated with better sleep quality; supplementation may help most when dietary intake is low.
The tolerable upper intake level for supplemental magnesium is 350 mg per day for adults; excess can cause diarrhea.
Magnesium can bind certain antibiotics and bisphosphonates and reduce their absorption; separate the doses by several hours.
L-theanine is an amino acid found in tea leaves, associated with a calm, relaxed state without strong sedation.
Typical study doses range from 200 to 400 mg, sometimes taken before bed to support relaxation.
L-theanine is often combined with magnesium or used to soften the stimulating effect of caffeine.
Glycine is an amino acid; small trials suggest about 3 grams before bed may improve subjective sleep quality.
Reported benefits include falling asleep faster and reduced next-day fatigue, though the evidence is preliminary.
Glycine may reduce the effectiveness of the antipsychotic clozapine.
Valerian is a traditional herbal sleep aid; clinical evidence is mixed and study quality varies.
Common doses are 300 to 600 mg of extract taken 30 minutes to two hours before bed.
Valerian can add to the sedative effects of benzodiazepines, sedative-hypnotics, and other CNS depressants.
Ashwagandha is an adaptogenic herb; root-extract trials suggest possible improvements in stress and sleep measures.
Studied doses commonly range from 250 to 600 mg of root extract per day.
Ashwagandha is not recommended during pregnancy and may affect thyroid hormone levels and immune activity.
